Integrity compliance To engage in business with the Government of Canada, businesses must adhere to strict integrity requirements. These requirements are designed to ensure ethical conduct, transparency and accountability throughout the procurement process. Office of Supplier Integrity and Compliance The Government of Canada established a comprehensive Integrity Regime to strengthen the integrity of procurement and real property transactions. In 2024, with the implementation of an enhanced program and updated Ineligibility and Suspension Policy, the Regime was replaced with the Office of Supplier Integrity and Compliance (OSIC). The Office fosters ethical business practices, ensures due process for offerors and contractors and upholds the public trust in the procurement process. This fosters public trust by adhering to rigorous and transparent practices that align with both national and international best practices. The OSIC is administered by Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC) under the authority of the Department of Public Works and Government Services Act and the Financial Administration Act as well as through the operation of Memoranda of Understanding that have been concluded with other federal departments, agencies and certain crown corporations. The OSIC administers the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy and the associated clauses that apply to various types of contracts, ensuring that all procurement activities are conducted under strict ethical standards. Ineligibility and Suspension Policy The Ineligibility and Suspension Policy outlines when and how a supplier can be declared ineligible or suspended from doing business with the federal government. This policy applies to all supply, construction, real property and service contracts. The policy includes new items that expand consideration of business ethics and corporate social responsibility as grounds for suspension or ineligibility. The policy also provides greater flexibility to respond proportionately to the identified risk. The Ineligibility and Suspension Policy provides mechanisms such as Public Interest Exceptions and Administrative Agreements, allowing contracts with entities that might otherwise be ineligible. This flexibility ensures that the government can maintain critical services and supplies while upholding ethical standards. Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses The Ineligibility and Suspension Policy lists some contracts that are exempt, but all solicitation documents and contracts must include the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses. Since the clauses incorporate the Policy by reference, any relevant exclusion will automatically apply. A request for changes to the clauses or for full or partial exemption from the policy requires escalation to the Assistant Deputy Minister, Procurement Branch via the appropriate Acquisitions Program policy contact . The following must be provided as part of the solicitation process: for a sole proprietor , the name of the owner for a private corporation , the names of all directors and the names of all individuals or entities that hold 5% or more of ownership for a public corporation , the names of all directors for a non-profit , the names of all directors for a general partnership , the names of all of the partners for a limited partnership (LP) or a limited liability partnership (LLP) , the names of all the general partners, and if the general partner is a public or non-profit corporation, the names of all directors if the general partner is a private corporation, the names of all of the directors, and the names of all individuals or entities that hold 5% or more of ownership for a trust , the names of the trustees, and: if the trustee is a public or non-profit corporation, the names of all directors if the trustee is a private corporation, the names of all of the directors and the names of all individuals or entities that hold 5% or more of ownership Integrity verification process Before awarding a contract (including issuing a purchase order), issuing a standing offer (SO) or supply arrangement (SA), or publishing of a pre-qualified supplier list, contracting officers must first verify with the Registrar of Ineligibility and Suspension (the Registrar) that the offeror is not ineligible to be awarded a contract or suspended. Certain charges, convictions and other circumstances, as described in the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy will or may render the offeror ineligible to be awarded or suspended from being awarded a contract. Before awarding any contract (including issuing a purchase order with a transaction value of $10,000 or more, including GST/HST), issuing a standing offer (SO), supply arrangement (SA), or publishing a pre-qualified businesses list, you must verify through the Registrar of Ineligibility and Suspensions (The Registrar) that the offeror or contractor is eligible and not suspended. You must submit the offeror or contractor details to the Registrar via the Integrity Verification Services (IVS) (accessible only on the government of Canada network) portal for contracts exceeding $10,000 (GST/HST included). This includes a list of names received from the offeror with their offer as per Section 13 of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy . When submitting an Integrity Verification request, you must certify that the information provided to the IVS is as received from the offeror. If additional information is required to determine the offeror’s status, IVS will request it from you. You must then inform the offeror of their obligation to provide the information within a recommended 10 business days. Failure to provide this information within the stipulated timeframe will render the offer non-compliant. Verification responses Two responses are possible, as described below. buyers are to keep a copy of the confirmation email from Integrity Verification Services in the procurement file. This process and the estimated timelines can be delayed should additional information be required by IVS . “ Integrity result – The offeror is not ineligible. (No match) ”: Allows you to proceed with contract award, issuance of the SO or SA , or publication of the list of pre-qualified suppliers. “ Ineligibility confirmed ”: Confirming the offeror as ineligible or suspended. The offeror is suspended or ineligible to be awarded a contract or issued a standing offer or supply arrangement, or to be included in a list of pre-qualified businesses. In exceptional circumstances, you may request a Public Interest Exception (PIE) to enter into a contract with an ineligible supplier. In cases of "Ineligibility confirmed" consult with the appropriate Acquisitions Program contact. Exceptions to verification requirement You are not required to perform any verification in the following circumstances: call-ups against standing offers contracts against supply arrangements, unless the SA was issued prior to April 4, 2016, and includes the latest Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses task authorizations (TAs) contract amendments, unless changes to the Integrity Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses or board members are involved purchases made with acquisition cards contracts under $10,000.00 (GST/HST included) other specified contracts or contract types as detailed in the policy Amendments and ongoing contracts For existing contracts that do not contain current Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses, you must propose to integrate the clauses during contract renewals or amendments. You may use the appropriate template letter for integrity compliance. If a contractor refuses the updated clauses, you should ensure that the contractor clearly understands the updates. This may involve having a manager contact the contractor directly. Continued refusal does not halt business operations; contract amendments may still proceed. If the contractor accepts the new clauses, you must follow the verification process outlined above if the existing contract contains Ineligibility and Suspension Clauses dated prior to July 2015. You must also follow the verification process outlined above for contract amendments if either of the following apply: the file does not contain a record of a previous verification the information required to be provided under Section 13 of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy has changed If a contractor is found ineligible or suspended during an active contract, the Registrar will notify you. Decisions on contract termination will be coordinated with the Acquisitions Program, and the contractor may need to enter into an Administrative Agreement with PSPC . For assistance with the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses and their application, consult the appropriate Acquisitions Program policy contact. Administrative agreements Administrative agreements are managed by the Departmental Oversight Branch. They are negotiated agreements between Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) and the contractor, current or potential, to, among other things: suspend the period of ineligibility to contract with Canada avoid a suspension enter into a contract where the Public Interest Exception (PIE) is invoked and where time is not of the essence, or continue a contract where the contracting authority has a right to terminate the contract due to non-compliance with the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy Additional information on Administrative Agreements can be found in Section 10 titled Administrative Agreements of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy. Applying Public Interest Exceptions The Registrar and the relevant Deputy Head or equivalent may allow for the award of a contract to an ineligible or suspended offeror where public interest exceptions (PIE) apply per Section 9 of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy. Further to Section 9 of the Policy, the buyer must escalate the request to their Deputy head or equivalent for recommendation to the Registrar, prior to award of the contract. The Registrar may require a supplier to enter into an administrative agreement before being awarded a contract further to PIE . Note : PIE cannot be applied if the supplier has no capacity to contract with Canada and any other person pursuant to, subsection 750(3) of the Criminal Code or is otherwise prohibited from contracting with Canada pursuant to a sanction listed in paragraph (1)(i) of Appendix 2 of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy . Allowing continuation of contracts This may enable the continuation of existing contracts in situations where you might otherwise have grounds to terminate due to breaches of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy . Contracting with subcontractors When contracting with the Government of Canada, contractors understand that they cannot enter into a contract with first-tier subcontractors that have been determined to be ineligible or suspended under the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy . A first-tier subcontract is defined in the Policy as “a contract made between the offeror or contractor and a person (other than Canada) for carrying out or supplying goods for part or all of another contract between the supplier and Canada (which may include, without limitation, all the activities, services, goods, matters and things required to be done, delivered or performed by the supplier under a contract or real property agreement); and, a sublease made between the supplier and a person (other than Canada) for part of or all of premises leased by Canada to the supplier.” A first-tier subcontractor is defined in the Policy as a contractor to a first-tier subcontract. Verification responsibilities Offerors are required to verify the eligibility status of first-tier subcontractors before submitting offers and prior to forming any contractual relationships. Approval for contracting with ineligible subcontractors If a need arises to subcontract with an ineligible or suspended entity, contractors must obtain prior written approval from the appropriate deputy head or their delegate. This is accomplished by forwarding this request to you the buyer and providing a copy of the request to the Registrar . Contractors who enter into a first-tier subcontract with an ineligible or suspended first-tier subcontractor without the prior written consent from the relevant deputy head can be declared ineligible in accordance with the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy. Subcontractor replacement If a successful offeror intends to replace a now ineligible or suspended first-tier subcontractor before contract award, a rigorous re-evaluation process that ensures that all offerors are treated fairly and equally must be followed. This process is adjusted based on specific procurement scenarios. See Replacement entity in Appendix 1: Definitions of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy . Procedural requirements Any requests to replace an ineligible or suspended first-tier subcontractor must be directed to the appropriate Acquisitions Program contact. During extended offer evaluations, you must remind the successful offeror, using the template provided by the Acquisitions Program contact, to do one of the following: obtain PSPC approval before contracting with an ineligible or suspended first-tier subcontractor replace any first-tier subcontractor who has become ineligible or suspended since the offer was originally submitted, as long as Canada accepts the replacement More detailed information on dealing with ineligible or suspended subcontractors is available in Section 12 of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy . Legal entity Whatever the form of procurement document, the buyer must ensure that it is with a business that is a legal entity having the legal capacity to contract, and that the business legal name is used. The legal name of the business must be used in procurement documents. In situations where the business operates under a different name, the legal name must still be used. For instance, a numbered company doing business under a different name should have its legal name (the number of the company) listed, followed by "doing business as" with the business name specified. Note : Possessing a business number does not equate to having the legal capacity to contract. The buyer must still verify that the supplier has the legal capacity to contract. Divisions within a corporation are not recognized as separate legal entities and cannot be awarded a contract. In cases where an abbreviated name is used, you should confirm that it is the correct legal entity name by checking relevant source lists for accuracy and completeness. Legal status of Indigenous business Identifying a business as an Indigenous business does not alter its requirement to be a legal entity capable of entering into enforceable contracts with Canada. If there is any uncertainty about the legal status of an Indigenous business, you are required to consult with legal counsel to confirm if it can legally engage in a contract. This step ensures the validity and enforceability of the contract. Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business For procurement designated under Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business (PSIB), an offeror’s certification as an Indigenous business may be subject to audit to confirm its validity. The certification includes an undertaking that the business will continue to meet the criteria, which define it as Indigenous throughout the performance of the contract. An offeror/supplier’s certification that it is Indigenous is subject to audit, both before and after contract award. Pre-award audit of the offeror’s certification For requirements valued at $2 million or more pre-award audit of the offeror’s certification is mandatory. To meet this mandatory pre-award audit requirement, you must ensure that Indigenous Services Canada (ISC) is properly notified according to established guidelines. You must also submit the top two best-assessed offers to ISC for confirmation of eligibility before contract award. You must not award contracts of $2 million or more until ISC has confirmed the eligibility of the proposed contractor. For contracts under $2 million, a random pre-award audit may be conducted. ISC will inform you whether a pre-award audit is necessary no later than the solicitation closing date. Typically, these audits take about 10 working days. If timing of the contract award is critical, you should communicate this information to ISC in order to have the audit process expedited, or determine if the procurement can be excluded from random selection. Pre-award audits may be initiated by the requisitioning authority, you or ISC if there is doubt about the validity of offerors’ certifications, regardless of the procurement value. If ISC advises that the requirement will be subject to a pre-award audit, you should proceed with the evaluation of offers until the top two “best-assessed” offers are identified. You must provide this information, excluding pricing details, to ISC for the pre-award audit. ISC will advise you once the audit results are received. The audit outcome may: validate the offeror’s certifications, in which case the contract may be awarded reveal invalid certifications, in which case the offeror(s) with invalid certifications will be declared non-compliant, and the next-ranked offeror may be advanced and referred to ISC for audit reveal all offerors are found to be non-compliant, then the solicitation of offers may need to be reissued with or without a set-aside after consulting with the client and evaluating this decision on sound contracting principles and on a case-by-case basis

All requirements except construction

Lapsing of surety bonds or return of security deposits (government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, irrevocable standby letters of credit) must occur in the following way for all requirements except construction:

for all offerors, at the expiration of the offer validity period, either as originally set or as extended

for unsuccessful offerors, prompt notification or return of a security deposit, immediately after a contract is awarded, is essential so as not to constrain the their ability to make new offeror

for a successful offeror, if no contract security is required, immediately upon award of a contract or, if contract security is required, once the contract security is received

Construction requirements

Lapsing of surety bonds, or return of security deposits (government guaranteed bonds, bills of exchange, irrevocable standby letters of credit) for construction requirements must occur as soon as practical following:

the solicitation of offers closing date, for those offerors submitting non-compliant offers

the administrative offer review, for those offerors submitting compliant offers ranked fourth to last on the schedule of offers

the award of contract, for those offerors submitting the second and third ranked offers

the receipt of contract security from the successful offeror or the cancellation of the solicitation of offers, for all offerors

If one or more of the offers ranked third to first is withdrawn or rejected for whatever reason, then Canada should reserve the right to hold the offer security of the next highest ranked compliant offer in order to retain the offer security of at least three valid and compliant offers

Office of Supplier Integrity and Compliance

The Government of Canada established a comprehensive Integrity Regime to strengthen the integrity of procurement and real property transactions. The OSIC administers the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy and the associated clauses that apply to various types of contracts, ensuring that all procurement activities are conducted under strict ethical standards.

Ineligibility and Suspension Policy

The Ineligibility and Suspension Policy outlines when and how a supplier can be declared ineligible or suspended from doing business with the federal government. This policy applies to all supply, construction, real property and service contracts.

The policy includes new items that expand consideration of business ethics and corporate social responsibility as grounds for suspension or ineligibility. The policy also provides greater flexibility to respond proportionately to the identified risk. The Ineligibility and Suspension Policy provides mechanisms such as Public Interest Exceptions and Administrative Agreements, allowing contracts with entities that might otherwise be ineligible. This flexibility ensures that the government can maintain critical services and supplies while upholding ethical standards.

Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses

The Ineligibility and Suspension Policy lists some contracts that are exempt, but all solicitation documents and contracts must include the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses. Since the clauses incorporate the Policy by reference, any relevant exclusion will automatically apply. A request for changes to the clauses or for full or partial exemption from the policy requires escalation to the Assistant Deputy Minister, Procurement Branch via the appropriate Acquisitions Program policy contact .

The following must be provided as part of the solicitation process:

for a sole proprietor , the name of the owner

for a private corporation , the names of all directors and the names of all individuals or entities that hold 5% or more of ownership

for a public corporation , the names of all directors

for a non-profit , the names of all directors

for a general partnership , the names of all of the partners

for a limited partnership (LP) or a limited liability partnership (LLP) , the names of all the general partners, and if the general partner is a public or non-profit corporation, the names of all directors if the general partner is a private corporation, the names of all of the directors, and the names of all individuals or entities that hold 5% or more of ownership

if the general partner is a public or non-profit corporation, the names of all directors

if the general partner is a private corporation, the names of all of the directors, and the names of all individuals or entities that hold 5% or more of ownership

for a trust , the names of the trustees, and: if the trustee is a public or non-profit corporation, the names of all directors if the trustee is a private corporation, the names of all of the directors and the names of all individuals or entities that hold 5% or more of ownership

if the trustee is a public or non-profit corporation, the names of all directors

if the trustee is a private corporation, the names of all of the directors and the names of all individuals or entities that hold 5% or more of ownership

Integrity verification process

Before awarding a contract (including issuing a purchase order), issuing a standing offer (SO) or supply arrangement (SA), or publishing of a pre-qualified supplier list, contracting officers must first verify with the Registrar of Ineligibility and Suspension (the Registrar) that the offeror is not ineligible to be awarded a contract or suspended. Certain charges, convictions and other circumstances, as described in the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy will or may render the offeror ineligible to be awarded or suspended from being awarded a contract.

Before awarding any contract (including issuing a purchase order with a transaction value of $10,000 or more, including GST/HST), issuing a standing offer (SO), supply arrangement (SA), or publishing a pre-qualified businesses list, you must verify through the Registrar of Ineligibility and Suspensions (The Registrar) that the offeror or contractor is eligible and not suspended. You must submit the offeror or contractor details to the Registrar via the Integrity Verification Services (IVS) (accessible only on the government of Canada network) portal for contracts exceeding $10,000 (GST/HST included).

This includes a list of names received from the offeror with their offer as per Section 13 of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy .

When submitting an Integrity Verification request, you must certify that the information provided to the IVS is as received from the offeror. If additional information is required to determine the offeror’s status, IVS will request it from you. You must then inform the offeror of their obligation to provide the information within a recommended 10 business days. Failure to provide this information within the stipulated timeframe will render the offer non-compliant.

Verification responses

Two responses are possible, as described below. buyers are to keep a copy of the confirmation email from Integrity Verification Services in the procurement file. This process and the estimated timelines can be delayed should additional information be required by IVS .

“ Integrity result – The offeror is not ineligible. (No match) ”: Allows you to proceed with contract award, issuance of the SO or SA , or publication of the list of pre-qualified suppliers.

“ Ineligibility confirmed ”: Confirming the offeror as ineligible or suspended. The offeror is suspended or ineligible to be awarded a contract or issued a standing offer or supply arrangement, or to be included in a list of pre-qualified businesses. In exceptional circumstances, you may request a Public Interest Exception (PIE) to enter into a contract with an ineligible supplier.

In cases of "Ineligibility confirmed" consult with the appropriate Acquisitions Program contact.

Exceptions to verification requirement

You are not required to perform any verification in the following circumstances:

call-ups against standing offers

contracts against supply arrangements, unless the SA was issued prior to April 4, 2016, and includes the latest Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses

task authorizations (TAs)

contract amendments, unless changes to the Integrity Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses or board members are involved

purchases made with acquisition cards

contracts under $10,000.00 (GST/HST included)

other specified contracts or contract types as detailed in the policy

Amendments and ongoing contracts

For existing contracts that do not contain current Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses, you must propose to integrate the clauses during contract renewals or amendments. You may use the appropriate template letter for integrity compliance. If a contractor refuses the updated clauses, you should ensure that the contractor clearly understands the updates. This may involve having a manager contact the contractor directly. Continued refusal does not halt business operations; contract amendments may still proceed. If the contractor accepts the new clauses, you must follow the verification process outlined above if the existing contract contains Ineligibility and Suspension Clauses dated prior to July 2015.

You must also follow the verification process outlined above for contract amendments if either of the following apply:

the file does not contain a record of a previous verification

the information required to be provided under Section 13 of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy has changed

If a contractor is found ineligible or suspended during an active contract, the Registrar will notify you. Decisions on contract termination will be coordinated with the Acquisitions Program, and the contractor may need to enter into an Administrative Agreement with PSPC .

For assistance with the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy clauses and their application, consult the appropriate Acquisitions Program policy contact.

Administrative agreements

Administrative agreements are managed by the Departmental Oversight Branch. They are negotiated agreements between Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) and the contractor, current or potential, to, among other things:

suspend the period of ineligibility to contract with Canada

avoid a suspension

enter into a contract where the Public Interest Exception (PIE) is invoked and where time is not of the essence, or

continue a contract where the contracting authority has a right to terminate the contract due to non-compliance with the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy

Additional information on Administrative Agreements can be found in Section 10 titled Administrative Agreements of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy.

The Registrar and the relevant Deputy Head or equivalent may allow for the award of a contract to an ineligible or suspended offeror where public interest exceptions (PIE) apply per Section 9 of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy. Further to Section 9 of the Policy, the buyer must escalate the request to their Deputy head or equivalent for recommendation to the Registrar, prior to award of the contract. The Registrar may require a supplier to enter into an administrative agreement before being awarded a contract further to PIE .

Note : PIE cannot be applied if the supplier has no capacity to contract with Canada and any other person pursuant to, subsection 750(3) of the Criminal Code or is otherwise prohibited from contracting with Canada pursuant to a sanction listed in paragraph (1)(i) of Appendix 2 of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy .

This may enable the continuation of existing contracts in situations where you might otherwise have grounds to terminate due to breaches of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy .

Contracting with subcontractors

When contracting with the Government of Canada, contractors understand that they cannot enter into a contract with first-tier subcontractors that have been determined to be ineligible or suspended under the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy . A first-tier subcontract is defined in the Policy as “a contract made between the offeror or contractor and a person (other than Canada) for carrying out or supplying goods for part or all of another contract between the supplier and Canada (which may include, without limitation, all the activities, services, goods, matters and things required to be done, delivered or performed by the supplier under a contract or real property agreement); and, a sublease made between the supplier and a person (other than Canada) for part of or all of premises leased by Canada to the supplier.”

A first-tier subcontractor is defined in the Policy as a contractor to a first-tier subcontract.

Offerors are required to verify the eligibility status of first-tier subcontractors before submitting offers and prior to forming any contractual relationships.

If a need arises to subcontract with an ineligible or suspended entity, contractors must obtain prior written approval from the appropriate deputy head or their delegate. This is accomplished by forwarding this request to you the buyer and providing a copy of the request to the Registrar . Contractors who enter into a first-tier subcontract with an ineligible or suspended first-tier subcontractor without the prior written consent from the relevant deputy head can be declared ineligible in accordance with the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy.

If a successful offeror intends to replace a now ineligible or suspended first-tier subcontractor before contract award, a rigorous re-evaluation process that ensures that all offerors are treated fairly and equally must be followed. This process is adjusted based on specific procurement scenarios. See Replacement entity in Appendix 1: Definitions of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy .

Any requests to replace an ineligible or suspended first-tier subcontractor must be directed to the appropriate Acquisitions Program contact. During extended offer evaluations, you must remind the successful offeror, using the template provided by the Acquisitions Program contact, to do one of the following:

obtain PSPC approval before contracting with an ineligible or suspended first-tier subcontractor

replace any first-tier subcontractor who has become ineligible or suspended since the offer was originally submitted, as long as Canada accepts the replacement

More detailed information on dealing with ineligible or suspended subcontractors is available in Section 12 of the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy .

Legal entity

Whatever the form of procurement document, the buyer must ensure that it is with a business that is a legal entity having the legal capacity to contract, and that the business legal name is used.

The legal name of the business must be used in procurement documents. In situations where the business operates under a different name, the legal name must still be used. For instance, a numbered company doing business under a different name should have its legal name (the number of the company) listed, followed by "doing business as" with the business name specified.

Note : Possessing a business number does not equate to having the legal capacity to contract. The buyer must still verify that the supplier has the legal capacity to contract.

Divisions within a corporation are not recognized as separate legal entities and cannot be awarded a contract.

In cases where an abbreviated name is used, you should confirm that it is the correct legal entity name by checking relevant source lists for accuracy and completeness.

Legal status of Indigenous business

Identifying a business as an Indigenous business does not alter its requirement to be a legal entity capable of entering into enforceable contracts with Canada. If there is any uncertainty about the legal status of an Indigenous business, you are required to consult with legal counsel to confirm if it can legally engage in a contract. This step ensures the validity and enforceability of the contract.

Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business

For procurement designated under Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business (PSIB), an offeror’s certification as an Indigenous business may be subject to audit to confirm its validity. The certification includes an undertaking that the business will continue to meet the criteria, which define it as Indigenous throughout the performance of the contract. An offeror/supplier’s certification that it is Indigenous is subject to audit, both before and after contract award.

Pre-award audit of the offeror’s certification

For requirements valued at $2 million or more pre-award audit of the offeror’s certification is mandatory. To meet this mandatory pre-award audit requirement, you must ensure that Indigenous Services Canada (ISC) is properly notified according to established guidelines. You must also submit the top two best-assessed offers to ISC for confirmation of eligibility before contract award. You must not award contracts of $2 million or more until ISC has confirmed the eligibility of the proposed contractor.

For contracts under $2 million, a random pre-award audit may be conducted. ISC will inform you whether a pre-award audit is necessary no later than the solicitation closing date. Typically, these audits take about 10 working days. If timing of the contract award is critical, you should communicate this information to ISC in order to have the audit process expedited, or determine if the procurement can be excluded from random selection.

Pre-award audits may be initiated by the requisitioning authority, you or ISC if there is doubt about the validity of offerors’ certifications, regardless of the procurement value. If ISC advises that the requirement will be subject to a pre-award audit, you should proceed with the evaluation of offers until the top two “best-assessed” offers are identified. You must provide this information, excluding pricing details, to ISC for the pre-award audit. ISC will advise you once the audit results are received.

The audit outcome may:

validate the offeror’s certifications, in which case the contract may be awarded

reveal invalid certifications, in which case the offeror(s) with invalid certifications will be declared non-compliant, and the next-ranked offeror may be advanced and referred to ISC for audit

reveal all offerors are found to be non-compliant, then the solicitation of offers may need to be reissued with or without a set-aside after consulting with the client and evaluating this decision on sound contracting principles and on a case-by-case basis
